explicit_text: I grew up my entire life in Everett, Washington, raised by my aunt and uncle who were Greek immigrants. Everett was a milltown with five mills, and it was really a union town where many of my youth friends' parents were involved in unions. I went to Everett High School and graduated in 1961. It was a very nice community where I played sports and helped my uncle clean the Everett Trust and Savings Bank after school.
implicit_text: I grew up my entire life in a milltown in Washington State, raised by my aunt and uncle who were Greek immigrants. It was a very nice community where I played sports and helped my uncle clean the local trust and savings bank after school.

explicit_text: My uncle, who was a shoemaker, belonged to a union that I believe was affiliated with the AFL-CIO because when he died in 1977, we went to the AFL-CIO union hall in Everett to collect a $500 death benefit. Although he didn’t talk much about union issues, the AFL-CIO was part of the union structure in Everett, which was a strong union town. Later on, I became more involved in union activities myself.

Although he didn’t talk much about union issues, that federation was part of the union structure in my town, which was a strong union town. Later on, I became more involved in union activities myself.

explicit_text: I served in the US Army from 1962 to 1968 during the Vietnam War era, mostly as a reservist on standby. My best friend went through OCS, became a captain, and served two tours in Vietnam. The war was confusing to me politically, and I remember people protesting and calling us 'killers' while we were building a bridge over the Seattle Coliseum fountain. I was working at Scott Paper Company then and was more of a weekend warrior, so I didn’t have a strong opinion on whether the war was good or bad.

explicit_text: After high school, I worked for Scott Paper Company for two years while attending Everett Junior College. This was around the early 1960s before I moved to Anchorage, Alaska. I was working there when I was also a weekend warrior in the Army reserves during the Vietnam War, which made for an interesting balance between civilian work and military duties.

explicit_text: In 1964, I moved to Anchorage, Alaska, where my uncle owned a construction company. I worked in construction there until 1967, doing jobs like building officers' quarters at Elmendorf Air Force Base and piledriving for transform towers around Cook Inlet. Anchorage was a busy place for construction back then, and I was part of the Laborers' International Union while working there.

entity: Laborers' International Union of North America
explicit_text: While working construction in Alaska, I had to pay union dues to the Laborers' International Union of North America. It was different from the union I later joined because they just collected dues and there weren’t union meetings or much involvement. I was about 22 then and didn’t know much about union activities, but it was part of the construction industry in Anchorage and surrounding areas.

We were involved in heavy construction projects like piledriving and installing transform towers. The Kenai Peninsula was part of the broader area where construction was booming in the mid-1960s.

explicit_text: My first longshore job was on May 28, 1967, on the Kanoshima Maru, which was a log ship. It was a tough introduction to longshoring because the logs came in one or two at a time as they grew, and the work was noisy and dangerous with wires and logs moving around. I learned the job from experienced men in a Port Gamble gang, and despite the challenges, it turned out to be the greatest thing I ever did.

explicit_text: I worked in a Port Gamble gang when I started my longshore career, and the men there taught me how to handle the dangerous log ships. Port Gamble was part of the Everett area and had a strong union presence. The experience I gained there was foundational for my long career as a longshoreman.

explicit_text: While working on the docks, we handled a lot of pulp bales from Weyerhaeuser, which were heavy 450-pound bales. We used hooks to wing them up and stow them carefully. Weyerhaeuser was one of the major companies whose cargo we handled, along with logs, lumber, steel, and module homes.

explicit_text: I became more aware of labor history and union activities when I started coming to San Francisco around 1986 as a caucus delegate. It was there that I read about the long tradition of labor activity in Everett and the broader West Coast. San Francisco was important for union meetings and learning about the labor movement.

entity: 1971 International Longshore and Warehouse Union strike
explicit_text: I remember the 1971 ILWU strike well because there was no work during that time. It was a significant event that affected all of us longshoremen. The strike was part of the ongoing labor struggles on the West Coast, although I didn’t go into much detail about it at the time.

explicit_text: I served in the United States Army from 1962 to 1968, mostly as a reservist during the Vietnam War era. My military specialty was bridge building, although I started in a tank brigade. I did basic training at Fort Ord, California, and Fort Knox, Kentucky, and later joined a reserve bridge company back home in Everett.

explicit_text: I did two months of basic training at Fort Ord, California, when I first joined the Army in the early 1960s. It was part of my initial military training before moving on to Fort Knox. Fort Ord was where I began my military experience during the Vietnam War period.
